channel.title: Gradient Descent
channel.description: Daily AI intelligence briefing — data science, finance, sports, and research.

item.title: Gradient Descent — Monday, June 1, 2026
item.description: Think of AI models like chefs who learned to make dishes from either cookbooks or food chemistry textbooks - their recommendations will be vastly different. This difference in training data has a surprising echo in finance, where investors are reeval
item.content:encoded:
Gradient Descent · Monday, June 1, 2026 · 7 articles · ~3 min read

Today's Brief

Think of AI models like chefs who learned to make dishes from either cookbooks or food chemistry textbooks - their recommendations will be vastly different. This difference in training data has a surprising echo in finance, where investors are reevaluating their stock screening criteria and portfolio allocations based on shifting market sentiments. As you consider how AI tools are being used and misused, what's the one assumption you're making about your own data that you should revisit today?

📊 AI & Data Tools

Ask AI what goes with chicken and the answer depends on whether it learned from recipes or molecules

Researchers trained AI models on 4.14 million recipes in seven languages and the FlavorDB flavor database, resulting in different recommendations based on the training data. This changes the recipe suggestion tool, allowing developers to choose between recipe-based or molecule-based recommendations when building their own AI-powered cooking assistants. → Read

Anthropic bans AI tools during job interviews to see how candidates actually think

Anthropic's job interview process involves up to five rounds of testing without the use of AI tools, with salaries reaching up to $850,000. This changes the job interview workflow, requiring candidates to rely on their own skills and thinking rather than AI assistance, and potentially altering the way companies evaluate candidate potential. → Read

Anthropic study finds men use AI coding agents more than twice as often as women in social science research

An Anthropic study found that researchers with typically male names use AI coding agents more than twice as often as those with typically female names, with economists leading at 39 percent. This changes the decision of when to use AI coding agents in social science research, highlighting the need to address potential gender disparities in the adoption of these tools. → Read

📈 AI in Finance

Nvidia CEO Says Market Got it Wrong On Software Stocks | Insight with Haslinda Amin 6/1/2026

Nvidia's CEO claims the market has misjudged software stocks, potentially indicating a shift in investor sentiment. This changes the stock screening criteria investors should use to evaluate software companies today. → Read

Ten Cap's Liu on Markets, Investment Strategy

Jun Bei Liu, Co-founder and Lead Portfolio Manager at Ten Cap, shares her market outlook and investment strategy, offering insights for investors. This changes the portfolio allocation decisions investors should make based on Liu's analysis of current market trends. → Read

South Korea stocks hit fresh high amid mixed regional trade despite Trump's Iran deal caution

South Korea's stock market has reached a new high despite mixed regional trade and uncertainty surrounding US-Iran negotiations. This changes the country selection and regional allocation in investors' portfolios, particularly for those considering Asian markets. → Read

🎧 Podcasts

#497 – Biggest Mysteries in Physics: Antimatter, Dark Energy & ToE – Don Lincoln

Don Lincoln, a particle physicist at Fermilab, has spent decades working on high energy physics, tackling mysteries like antimatter and dark energy. This changes how physicists approach the Theory of Everything, a concept that could be reevaluated today by researchers and scientists in the field of physics. → Listen

Gradient Descent is free and automated. Browse the full archive or subscribe to get it every morning.

item.title: Gradient Descent — Sunday, May 31, 2026
item.description: Think of AI security like a game of telephone: one wrong whisper can throw off the whole conversation, and now attackers are using shared chat features to spread malware. This changes the way traders and sports teams approach data sharing, where a si

item.title: Gradient Descent — Friday, May 29, 2026
item.description: Think of today's AI advancements like a series of better tools in a workshop, each one making the others more useful: a sharper saw helps you build a better workbench, which in turn helps you craft an even sharper saw. As these improved AI tools spre

item.description: Think of today's stories like a master key that unlocks new rooms in different houses - the houses being data engineering, finance, and even travel writing. As you walk into the data engineering house, you notice how new tools like Langfuse and Webwr

item.description: Think of AI like a turbocharger for development workflows, where autonomous code optimization and bug detection are accelerating the pace of innovation, much like a sports team using data analytics to fine-tune their strategy. As AI models like Aliba

item.description: Think of AI advancements like a master key that can unlock and rewrite the rules in various domains, much like a browser extension can change how you interact with the web. As AI models outperform each other in areas like online interactions, finance

item.title: Gradient Descent — Friday, May 22, 2026
item.description: Think of AI models like high-performance sports cars: they used to require a huge team and a lot of resources to run, but now they can be driven by a much smaller team with fewer resources, like Cohere's new Command A+ model that can run on just two

item.title: Gradient Descent — Thursday, May 21, 2026
item.description: Think of today's stories like a sports team's playbook: just as a new play can change the entire game, new algorithms like Turbovec and Deepseek Code are rewriting the rules for data search and coding tools. On the trading desk, this means reevaluati

item.title: Gradient Descent — Wednesday, May 20, 2026
item.description: Think of AI models like athletes in training: they need the right coaching, equipment, and practice to improve, and now tools like Forge and Gemini 3.5 Flash are giving them a boost, allowing them to run faster and jump higher. On a trading desk, thi

item.title: Gradient Descent — Tuesday, May 19, 2026
item.description: Think of your personal data like a library book: you want to be able to check it out and use it without anyone knowing it's yours, which is basically what local reversible pseudonymization does. This concept is now being applied to cloud-hosted memor

item.title: Gradient Descent — Monday, May 18, 2026
item.description: Think of language models like bulky suitcases: the more you pack, the harder they are to move around, which is why researchers are excited about new tools to compress them without losing too much functionality, kind of like a travel-sized toiletry ba

item.title: Gradient Descent — Sunday, May 17, 2026
item.description: Think of AI like a master locksmith: it can now pick the locks of browser security, developing real exploits autonomously, and that changes the game for developers who need to test their defenses. On the trading desk, this means investors like Bill A

item.title: Gradient Descent — Saturday, May 16, 2026
item.description: Think of AI coding agents like sports players, each with their own strengths and weaknesses, and now we have a way to rank them based on actual performance metrics, like code quality and terminal benchmarks. This changes the game for trading desks, w

item.title: Gradient Descent — Friday, May 15, 2026
item.description: Think of AI models like high-performance sports cars - they can only go as fast as the road they're on, and right now, that road is the inference system. As investors and traders, you're likely watching the surge in stocks like Ford and Cerebras, but

item.title: Gradient Descent — Thursday, May 14, 2026
item.description: Think of training like a recipe: the right mix of ingredients and steps can drastically change the outcome, whether it's pre-training a language model or preparing for a marathon. As researchers and developers experiment with new methods, such as tok

item.title: Gradient Descent — Wednesday, May 13, 2026
item.description: Think of today's AI advancements like a master chef's kitchen, where memory is the key ingredient that makes all the difference - and now, hybrid-memory autonomous agents can whip up complex tasks with ease. This has significant implications for trad
